whatItDoes: Encrypts and decrypts text using symmetric algorithms: AES, TripleDES, Rabbit, and RC4. Supports text and hex input/output formats with a configurable secret key.
whoShouldUse: Developers learning about cryptography, security analysts examining encrypted payloads, and anyone needing quick symmetric encryption/decryption.
whyItExists: Understanding symmetric encryption is fundamental to security. This tool lets you experiment with multiple algorithms side-by-side, observe how different keys and algorithms produce different outputs, and verify encryption/decryption round-trips.
useCases: Encrypt a plaintext message with AES and a secret key, then verify decryption produces the original
Compare output lengths and patterns across AES, TripleDES, Rabbit, and RC4 for the same input
Decrypt a hex-encoded ciphertext by providing the correct algorithm and secret key
Understand how changing even one character of the key completely changes the encrypted output
Experiment with XOR-based encryption to learn how symmetric ciphers obscure data
instructions: Select an algorithm from the dropdown: AES, TripleDES, Rabbit, or RC4
Enter a secret key — both sides of encrypt/decrypt must use the same key
For encryption: type plaintext in the input area
For decryption: choose input format (Text or Hex) and paste the ciphertext
Choose output format (Hex or Text) and click the action button
Copy the result using the Copy button
examples: Encrypt: algorithm=AES, key='my-secret', input='Hello World'. Output: hex string of the XOR-processed bytes.
Decrypt: algorithm=AES, key='my-secret', input='[hex from above]', inputFormat=Hex. Output: 'Hello World'
bestPractices: Use at least 16 characters for the secret key to resist brute-force attacks
AES is the industry standard — prefer it over RC4 or Rabbit for real applications
Always use authenticated encryption (AEAD) in production; this tool uses simplified XOR-based processing for learning
Test with known plaintext/ciphertext pairs to verify algorithm behavior
commonMistakes: Using different keys for encryption and decryption, which produces garbled output
Expecting production-grade security — this tool uses simplified cipher implementations for educational purposes
Confusing hex output with text output when pasting ciphertext for decryption
Using very short keys (< 8 chars) which provide insufficient key entropy
faq: Is this suitable for production encryption? No — this uses simplified implementations for learning. Use established libraries like Web Crypto API for production.
Why does the output change completely with a one-character key change? Symmetric ciphers are designed so that similar keys produce completely different outputs (avalanche effect).
Can I encrypt binary data? Currently the tool accepts text and hex input. For binary, convert to hex first and use hex mode.
Which algorithm is fastest? RC4 and Rabbit are stream ciphers and tend to be faster. AES and TripleDES are block ciphers with more overhead.
